Curriculum → AI Engineer learning path → AI agents and autonomous systems → Tool use and function calling

Lesson 2 of 3

Tool use and function calling

Designing tool interfaces, function schemas, error handling, and safety guardrails for agents that interact with external systems.

25-35 min AI/ML flow AI agents and autonomous systems 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Tools transform LLMs from text generators into capable actors that can search, compute, access databases, and control external services.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Designing tool interfaces Function descriptions should explain when and why to use the tool2. Error handling and recovery Retry logic with exponential backoff handles transient failures3. Safety and permissions Principle of least privilege: agents should only access tools they need

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Tools transform LLMs from text generators into capable actors that can search, compute, access databases, and control external services. 2. Designing tool interfaces Function descriptions should explain when and why to use the tool3. Error handling and recovery Retry logic with exponential backoff handles transient failures4. Safety and permissions Principle of least privilege: agents should only access tools they need
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Tools transform LLMs from text generators into capable actors that can search, compute, access databases, and control external services.

What to say first

  • Can design tool schemas that minimize LLM invocation errors.
  • Implements proper error handling and fallback strategies for tool failures.
  • Applies safety guardrails appropriate to the risk level of each tool.
2

Study section

Designing tool interfaces

Clear function schemas, comprehensive descriptions, and well-typed parameters help LLMs select and invoke tools correctly.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Function descriptions should explain when and why to use the tool
  • Parameter schemas with types, constraints, and examples reduce invocation errors
  • Return value formatting affects how the LLM integrates tool results into reasoning
3

Study section

Error handling and recovery

Tools fail, return unexpected results, or timeout. Robust agents handle these gracefully and adapt their strategy.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Retry logic with exponential backoff handles transient failures
  • Fallback tools provide alternative paths when primary tools fail
  • The agent should explain failures to users rather than silently retrying forever
4

Study section

Safety and permissions

Tool access control, confirmation flows, and sandboxing prevent agents from taking dangerous actions.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Principle of least privilege: agents should only access tools they need
  • Human-in-the-loop confirmation for irreversible or high-stakes actions
  • Sandboxing prevents code execution tools from accessing sensitive resources

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can design tool schemas that minimize LLM invocation errors.
  • Implements proper error handling and fallback strategies for tool failures.
  • Applies safety guardrails appropriate to the risk level of each tool.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Vague tool descriptions that lead to incorrect tool selection.
  • Missing error handling causing agents to crash on tool failures.
  • Giving agents access to dangerous tools without confirmation gates.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Design a tool interface for an agent that manages cloud infrastructure.
  • How would you implement permission levels for an agent with database access?
  • What happens when a tool returns unexpected results? Design the recovery flow.

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can design tool schemas that minimize LLM invocation errors.
  • Implements proper error handling and fallback strategies for tool failures.
  • Applies safety guardrails appropriate to the risk level of each tool.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Vague tool descriptions that lead to incorrect tool selection.
  • Missing error handling causing agents to crash on tool failures.
  • Giving agents access to dangerous tools without confirmation gates.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • Design a tool interface for an agent that manages cloud infrastructure.
  • How would you implement permission levels for an agent with database access?
  • What happens when a tool returns unexpected results? Design the recovery flow.

Code example

Design function calling schemas

intermediate coding

Design and implement OpenAI-compatible function calling schemas for a set of tools. Include proper descriptions, parameter types, and required fields.

What to notice

  • OpenAI function schema uses JSON Schema format for parameters
  • Good descriptions help the LLM decide WHEN to use each tool
  • Required fields must be present; enum fields must match allowed values

Expected outcome

True for valid calls, False for missing required params or invalid enum values

Starter code 
import json
from typing import Dict, Any

# TODO: Define function schemas for these tools:
# 1. get_weather(city: str, units: "celsius"|"fahrenheit") -> weather info
# 2. send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str, urgent: bool = False)
# 3. search_database(query: str, table: str, limit: int = 10, filters: dict = {})

function_schemas = [
    # TODO: Define OpenAI-compatible function schema for each
]

def validate_function_call(name: str, arguments: Dict[str, Any], schemas: list) -> bool:
    """Validate that a function call matches its schema."""
    # TODO: Check required params, types, enum values
    pass

# Test
call = {"name": "get_weather", "arguments": {"city": "NYC", "units": "celsius"}}
print(validate_function_call(call["name"], call["arguments"], function_schemas))
Reference solution 
import json
from typing import Dict, Any

function_schemas = [
    {
        "name": "get_weather",
        "description": "Get current weather for a city. Use when user asks about weather conditions.",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "city": {"type": "string", "description": "City name"},
                "units": {"type": "string", "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"], "description": "Temperature unit"}
            },
            "required": ["city", "units"]
        }
    },
    {
        "name": "send_email",
        "description": "Send an email to a recipient. Use for communication tasks.",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "to": {"type": "string", "description": "Recipient email"},
                "subject": {"type": "string", "description": "Email subject"},
                "body": {"type": "string", "description": "Email body"},
                "urgent": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Mark as urgent", "default": False}
            },
            "required": ["to", "subject", "body"]
        }
    },
    {
        "name": "search_database",
        "description": "Search records in a database table with optional filters.",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "query": {"type": "string", "description": "Search query"},
                "table": {"type": "string", "description": "Table to search"},
                "limit": {"type": "integer", "description": "Max results", "default": 10},
                "filters": {"type": "object", "description": "Key-value filters"}
            },
            "required": ["query", "table"]
        }
    }
]

def validate_function_call(name, arguments, schemas):
    schema = next((s for s in schemas if s["name"] == name), None)
    if not schema:
        return False
    required = schema["parameters"].get("required", [])
    for param in required:
        if param not in arguments:
            return False
    props = schema["parameters"]["properties"]
    for key, value in arguments.items():
        if key in props and "enum" in props[key]:
            if value not in props[key]["enum"]:
                return False
    return True

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "Tool use and function calling" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • Design a tool interface for an agent that manages cloud infrastructure.
  • Designing tool interfaces
  • Error handling and recovery
  • Can design tool schemas that minimize LLM invocation errors.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
